summary: Julia Evans introduces dnspeep, a 250-line Rust tool she built that shows DNS queries and responses from your computer in a human-readable format. The tool uses libpcap to capture packets and matches queries with their responses automatically, solving the main usability problem with tcpdump's DNS output. She explains the motivation: DNS seems mysterious to people who can't easily observe it, and existing tools like tcpdump require too much expertise to parse. She walks through several implementation challenges including libpcap compatibility issues, static linking difficulties, and the surprisingly hard problem of naming a DNS tool when every spy-related word is taken.
key_points: DNS becomes less mysterious when you can actually watch the queries and responses your computer makes in real time
tcpdump can show DNS traffic but forces users to manually match requests with responses and parse dense output format
Watching DNS queries reveals which programs on your computer are secretly using the Internet, sometimes surfacing forgotten software
Building small, focused tools that present existing information more clearly can be more valuable than just explaining complex existing tools
Even a 250-line Rust program encounters substantial real-world problems: libpcap version differences across distros, crate compatibility bugs, and Ethernet frame parsing edge cases
money_quotes: I think DNS can seem really mysterious when you don't know a lot about it!
Your browser (and other software on your computer) is making DNS queries all the time, and I think it makes it seem a lot more 'real' when you can actually see the queries and responses.
I think the question 'is this a DNS problem?' is harder to answer than it should be.
A friend of mine actually discovered using this tool that he had some corporate monitoring software installed on his computer from an old job that he'd forgotten to uninstall.
That's the kind of thing computers are good at!
This was one of those bugs which took many hours to figure out and 1 line to fix :)
I'm interested in making more friendly ways to see the same information so that everyone can understand what DNS queries their computer is making instead of just tcpdump wizards :).
key_insight: Building simpler, friendlier interfaces to existing low-level information — like matching DNS queries to responses automatically — can make debugging accessible to everyone instead of just power users.
